coredata.dc:title: A highly effective and robust structure-based LSTM with feature-vector tuning framework for high-accuracy SOC estimation in EV
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ahn, Junyoung
Lee, Yoonseok
Han, Byeongjik
Lee, Sohyeon
Kim, Yunsun
Chung, Daewon
Jeon, Joonhyeon
coredata.dc:description: This paper describes a new dual long short-term memory (LSTM) model for accurate estimation of the state of charge (SOC) of lithium–ion batteries in electric vehicles. The proposed network has highly effective and robust structure combining a mainstream (m–) LSTM and gradient (g–) LSTM in parallel, which can capture both data-temporal dependency and variability in battery's time-series. The g–LSTM possessing a gradient function consists of very few unit-cells corresponding to about 3 % of m–LSTM cells, and helps prevent the decrease of SOC accuracy caused by sudden changes of current and voltage during charging and discharging. Experimental results show that due to the gradient-tuning effect of feature vectors, the proposed model offers an innovative approach to predicting the SOC patterns with extraordinary precision, resulting in remarkably improved accuracy, on average 12.02 % higher than that of the vanilla LSTM. Further, the proposed dual LSTM demonstrates a fast convergence speed in the training process, and achieves highly accurate SOC estimation, even on unexpected data. Consequently, the computationally efficient and effective g–LSTM collaboration provides a highly robust and strong LSTM network structure to accurately estimate battery SOC, which helps maintain stable performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: State of charge (SOC)
Battery management system (BMS)
SOC estimation
Deep learning
Lithium–ion battery
LSTM

Key viewpoints for the design of the LSTM-based network model
A dual LSTM network architecture in combination with g–LSTM
Determination of the number of g–LSTM unit cells over m–LSTM

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP), and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20224000000020). This work was also supported by the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT), Korea, under the ICT Challenge and Advanced Network of HRD (ICAN) support program (IITP-2024-RS-2024-00437186), supervised by the Institute for Information & Communications Technology Planning & Evaluation (IITP).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Voltage, current, and SOC profiles under temperatures of (a) 0 °C, (b) 25 °C, and (c) 45 °C.
(a) 0 °C, (b) 25 °C, and (c) 45 °C profiles and distribution graph plotted on the V−I plane.
LSTM cell structure.
Proposed dual LSTM network structure.
Structure of the g–LSTM cell containing g–function.
Stochastic distributions of the gradient features of (a) 0 °C, (b) 25 °C, and (c) 45 °C profiles and distribution graphs plotted on the V−I plane.
Detailed data flow of the dual LSTM.
Comparison of average errors computed for 10 runs of varying sequence length.
Performance comparison of the average SOC estimation errors according to a split number of 256 unit-cells in the dual–LSTM model with single layer.
Performance comparison of three deep-learning models for SOC estimation errors (a) MAE, (b) MSE, and (c) RMSE.
SOC estimation-curve comparison of the dual LSTM and vanilla LSTM models: (a)∼(b) are 1200 epochs, (c)∼(d) are 2400 epochs.
Specification of the tested battery.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ※ The computation time of the model varies according to the volume of input data being processed. Additionally, in the case of dual LSTM, it is predominantly determined by the number of mainstream (m-) LSTM cells.

head.ce:title: A highly effective and robust structure-based LSTM with feature-vector tuning framework for high-accuracy SOC estimation in EV

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Advanced Battery Convergence Engineering, Dongguk University, Seoul, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Advanced Battery Convergence Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Advanced Battery Convergence Engineering, Dongguk University-Seoul, Korea.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic and Electrical Engineering, Dongguk University, Seoul, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronic and Electrical Engineering, Dongguk University-Seoul, Korea.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Advanced Battery Convergence Engineering, Dongguk University, Seoul, South Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Advanced Battery Convergence Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes a new dual long short-term memory (LSTM) model for accurate estimation of the state of charge (SOC) of lithium–ion batteries in electric vehicles. The proposed network has highly effective and robust structure combining a mainstream (m–) LSTM and gradient (g–) LSTM in parallel, which can capture both data-temporal dependency and variability in battery's time-series. The g–LSTM possessing a gradient function consists of very few unit-cells corresponding to about 3 % of m–LSTM cells, and helps prevent the decrease of SOC accuracy caused by sudden changes of current and voltage during charging and discharging. Experimental results show that due to the gradient-tuning effect of feature vectors, the proposed model offers an innovative approach to predicting the SOC patterns with extraordinary precision, resulting in remarkably improved accuracy, on average 12.02 % higher than that of the vanilla LSTM. Further, the proposed dual LSTM demonstrates a fast convergence speed in the training process, and achieves highly accurate SOC estimation, even on unexpected data. Consequently, the computationally efficient and effective g–LSTM collaboration provides a highly robust and strong LSTM network structure to accurately estimate battery SOC, which helps maintain stable performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The reference is provided for the data used in this study.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth and transition of the energy storage device towards green energy has resulted in the lithium–ion battery (LIB) attracting extensive attention in the electric vehicle (EV) field, as a consequence of its high energy density, long cycle life, and low self-discharge rate [1–3]. However, overcharge and over-discharge can irreparably damage the LIB, sometimes giving rise to significant problems that include thermal runaway and thermal propagation caused by its intrinsic chemical properties [4]. For this reason, estimating the state-of-charge (SOC) of a battery state is considered the most important part of EVs, contributing to preventing battery failures in advance, and enabling efficient operation. This is because the estimation of SOC is crucial, in that it is closely related to assuring extended battery life, and preventing overcharge and over-discharge. Thus, the battery SOC should be accurately monitored and balanced in real-time. Nevertheless, accurate estimation of the SOC in EVs is very difficult, because the batteries have complex nonlinear electrochemical characteristics that depend significantly on time-varying parameters. Accordingly, it is still a challenging task to accurately estimate the battery SOC on-site, based on time-series data with dynamic variability. So far, the traditional SOC estimation methods that have been reported include coulomb counting [5,6], open-circuit-voltage (OCV) [7], and digital filter [8,9]. But they give rise to serious SOC prediction errors, due to the ever-changing current, voltage, temperature, and capacity variability in the real-time operating conditions of EVs on-site [9,10]. To overcome the difficulty of adapting to estimating the online SOC in EVs, data-driven methods have been reported, which provide advanced knowledge-based architectures for specific type of LIBs, and produce excellent results in various time-series data pattern recognition tasks [10–16]. The deep learning model can overcome these methodological difficulties in that it is automatically adaptive to the given linear and non-linear data patterns online, and can be optimized without manual modeling of the system. Among the many deep learning models published to date, long short-term memory (LSTM) is widely used for the accurate SOC estimation of battery in EVs, as one way to solve the problems of vanishing gradient and long-term dependency that exist in recurrent neural network (RNN) based on feature grouping [17–20]. This is because LSTM incorporates two feedback connections operating at different time scales, allowing it to process longer sequences of data, rather than just individual data points [21,22]. However, because of its complex and expressive structure, the LSTM often suffers from overfitting, which leads to lower-than-expected accuracy, due to the nonlinear dynamic response of battery to the drastic change of unlearned data [19].

Recently, to reduce the dependency of training data and further enhance the SOC accuracy, various approaches collaborating with LSTM have been presented [17,18,23,24]. Yang et al. [17] suggested a combination model adding Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) into LSTM−RNN to model battery behaviors and estimate the battery SOC under varying temperatures. Their proposed method showed a good tendency to correlate the battery SOC to the change in the temperature range (0–50) °C. Chen et al. [18] investigated a combined model of LSTM−RNN with extended input and constrained output, to solve the problem of fluctuations of the significant output SOC brought about by the direct mapping using fast time-varying input data. Fasahat et al. [23] reported the combinational network by adding the autoencoder (AE) in front of the LSTM, and used the AE to help the LSTM extract the accurate features of input data. This model made it possible to predict the trend of data more precisely at three different temperatures during two different driving cycles. Wang et al. [24] proposed a deep learning model that combined convolutional neural network (CNN) and LSTM as the core, and deployed coulomb counting and Kalman filter on the end-side to more accurately estimate SOC. This model effectively dealt with the time-series problem of SOC estimation in the battery, a non-linear system, and exhibited satisfactory estimation accuracy under different temperatures. The network on the unlearned data at different temperatures displayed satisfactory SOC estimation performance and generalization ability. Clearly, the combination of LSTM and different algorithms provides an effective solution to obtain the satisfactory estimation of SOC, but these collaborative approaches on learning have many drawbacks that include large computation consumption times and limited accuracy due to the random nonlinear dynamic response of the battery. The reason is that in their collaboration model, the characterizing role of each network on learning was not considered in relation to the data type and test conditions. In particular, in the case of unlearned data, the performance of the LSTM combination model is also highly dependent on the unexpected variability of the battery's nonlinear dynamic response, and thereby a fatal accuracy degradation often occurs, similar to that of the vanilla LSTM.

To address these challenges, this paper describes a novel dual mode LSTM network using a gradient-tuning (g–) LSTM module in parallel as an active way of coping with the imitations of imperceptible change and the dependency of training data. The g–LSTM learns the input-data dynamic variation information represented by a gradient (g–) function in its network, and its extracted gradient-variation features are incorporated into a dense block to assign weights to the output features of a m–LSTM. The key highlight of the proposed dual LSTM lies in its strong structure-based model of capturing both temporal dependencies and nonlinear dynamics inherent in battery time series data, which contributes to overcoming the greatest weakness of current and voltage nonlinearity causing large SOC estimation errors during charging and discharging. The superior dual-LSTM model, capturing and utilizing the temporal-dependency and nonlinear-variability features, stems from the stochastic characteristic analysis including auto- and cross-correlations of the time series data. Above all, it is experimentally proved that the proposed dual model leads to apparently high SOC estimation accuracy, notwithstanding that its computation parameters are fewer than those of the vanilla LSTM networks with the same number of unit cells. The successful collaboration of the g–LSTM consisting of very few unit-cells appears to prevent the decrease in SOC accuracy caused by sudden changes of current and voltage, and helps maintain its stable performance for the dynamic, random and nonlinear response of the battery. The proposed dual–LSTM collaboration enables the handling of a wide variety of tasks and the extraction of valuable insights out of all kinds of time-series information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Motivation/stochastic characteristics analysis of the battery's time-series data
ce:section.ce:para: The SOC estimation accuracy of data-driven neural network models is most affected by the stochastic characteristics and uncertainties of learned and unlearned input data, which are random nonlinearity, variation, instability, and non-consistency in the high-noise electric environment of batteries. To solve such problems, various collaborative approaches have been reported, as mentioned above. However, the exact stochastic analysis of the battery's time-series data in current and voltage space according to temperature is not yet sufficiently developed. For this reason, this session deals with the stochastic distribution of the time-series information, before suggesting a new deep learning model.

The actual driving conditions of the EV demonstrate high complexity and uncertainty in noisy electric environments. Considering these points, three dynamic driving profiles were chosen in this work, which are dynamic stress testing (DST), the US06 driving schedule (US06), and the federal urban driving schedule (FUDS). These datasets were designed by the United States Advanced Battery Consortium (USABC) under various EV driving conditions, and come from the public database (INR 18650-20R LiNiMnCo/Graphite battery) of the Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CALCE) [25]. Then, Table 1 describes the specification of the battery used. Fig. 1 shows the voltage, current, and SOC profiles under temperatures of (0, 25, and 45) °C, respectively. The current data reveals larger and more rapid dynamic variability than the voltage data. Thus, before designing a data-driven neural network architecture, it is necessary to investigate the stochastic properties of the input data.

Fig. 2 shows the stochastic characteristics of the battery datasets used in this work, which is represented with the current value I
t for a given voltage value V
t in the V−I coordinate plane. Table 2 shows the related data. Fig. 2 and Table 2 show that although the discharge process is executed from about 2.4 to 4.0 V, more than 80 % of the voltage values are located in the narrow range between 3.40 and 3.76 V, and are centralized around a mean voltage of about 3.58 V, regardless of the profile type and discharge temperature. Further, during discharge, the current values intensively and dynamically vary between −4.1 and 2.0 A, and more than 76.7 % of the current values are distributed in the dynamic range of −1.5–0.0A, which are represented along with the voltage values (>80 %) in the range of 2.5–3.4 V. In particular, Table 2 shows that the mean of the current data is much lower than that of the voltage (i.e., m
I = −0.53 less than m
V = 3.58), but its standard deviation (SD)
σ I
is greater, which is approximately 5.06 times higher than
σ V
. This implies that the variability of current is higher and more dynamic than that of voltage, and is significantly affected by the battery's profile type and operating conditions. In other words, the distribution and variability of current in the battery's times-series data is highly associated with its driving profile, unlike that of voltage dependence on the battery characteristics [26]. For example, the current samples of the DST profile have a relatively wide operating range and two-peak distribution, while those of the US06 profile reveal consistently smaller SD than that of the other two profiles. This implies that the current change denotes different stochastic characteristics that are dependent on the driving environment and the battery temperature. As such, the random dynamic variability of current in the time-series data can also be obviously explained through the autocorrelation analysis of Table 3 , where
ρ I
( τ )
and
ρ V
( τ )
denote the time-lag
τ
autocorrelations of the current and voltage, respectively, in the time-series data. These show that the current autocorrelation rapidly decreases with increasing time lag
τ
, while that of voltage slowly decreases, indicating that in the time-series data, the current variability is higher and more dynamic than that of voltage. In other words, this analysis result suggests that a new deep-learning model should capture both long-term temporal dependencies (of voltage) and nonlinear dynamics (of current) in the time series of battery output. Table 4 compares the cross-correlations to investigate fluctuation similarity (variability traces) of two different time-series, i.e., current–voltage, current–SOC, and voltage–SOC, where
ρ
V − I
( τ )
,
ρ
I − S O C
( τ )
, and
ρ
V − S O C
( τ )
denote the cross-correlations of the current, voltage, and SOC with time-lag
τ
in the time-series data, respectively. From Table 4, the two kinds of cross-correlations with respect to current are very much lower regardless of time lag, while the voltage displays relatively high cross-correlations with the SOC data. This is attributed to the larger and more dynamic variability of current than that of voltage in the time-series, while the high cross-correlation between voltage and SOC indicates that the voltage values have a significant impact on SOC prediction. Therefore, the battery's voltage on LSTM shows a prominent effect on characterizing the variability and predictability of the SOC. In particular, the high-incidence and large-magnitude voltage values centralized in the narrow range (3.40–3.76) V play an apparently leading role in the generation of output features, which is due to using minimum-error reinforcement learning for model optimization. On the other hand, low-incidence input features outside the major distribution range are completely excluded from learning. More importantly, regarding the irregular current data changing more dramatically and rapidly at the wide operating range of (−4.1 to 2.2) A, there is a great problem of learning without considering the dynamic variability of the data. This is because the current causes the voltage change in an EV battery that repeatedly charges and discharges on-site.

Consequently, these results suggest that such LSTM learning problems (high variability, like current as against voltage according to the nonlinear response of batteries) lead to significant trouble in capturing distinct output features in the battery's time-series data, and provide the fundamental cause of a decrease in the accuracy of SOC estimation. This is due to the battery's nonlinear and time-varying characteristics having negative direct impacts on the performance of SOC estimation. Therefore, the effects of current variability on voltage should be also considered on learning in order to reduce the SOC inaccuracy due to the dynamic nonlinearity between the profiles of current and voltage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Key viewpoints for the design of the LSTM-based network model
ce:section.ce:para: A long short-term memory (LSTM) network is one of the most well-known types of recurrent neural networks, as the strengths lie in its ability to model long-range dependencies, making it especially useful in time-series prediction. However, the LSTM network lacks the ability to capture the nonlinearity features from the battery's dynamic time-series data mentioned in section 2.2, resulting in significantly lower-than-expected accuracy for the drastic change of unlearned data. This is due to the problem of its complex and expressive structure being unsuited to capture the long-term dependencies of highly uncorrelated data. Accordingly, a LSTM combination model of a reliable and robust structure is needed that possesses a collaboration module that can extract the dynamic variability features on learning. Before proposing a new LSTM-based collaboration model, the traditional LSTM structure is summarized as follows:

Fig. 3 displays a vanilla LSTM cell structure with two outputs of the cell state and hidden state, which consists of forget, input, and output gates [27]. The cell state
C t
of time-step
t
represents the memory of the network, storing information over time, while the hidden state
h t
contains the information that is passed to the next time-step. Further, each of the three gates plays a specific role in the functioning of the LSTM cell, as indicated in Eqs. (1)–(5). In the equations, all of
f t
,
i t
, and
o t
denote the outputs of the three gates using a sigmoid function
σ
( · )
to output numbers ranging 0 to 1, with values closer to 1 indicating ‘keep this’, while closer to 0 indicating ‘discard this’ [28–31]. The
{
W
x ∗
,
W
h ∗
,
b ∗
|
∗ = f , t , n , o
}
also denotes the corresponding weights and bias that are updated through the LSTM network. The calculation process of the LSTM cell unit for Eqs. (1)–(5) is as follows. The input gate (outputting
i t
) calculates weights and bias
{
0 ≤
(
W
x i
W
h i
,
b i
)
≤ 1
}
based on the current input
x t
and previous-time hidden state
h
t − 1
, to decide the importance of
x t
, and store its new information into the current cell state
C t
. The forget gate (with the output of
f t
) also calculates weights and bias
{
0 ≤
(
W
x f
,
W
h f
,
b f
)
≤ 1
}
for the given
x t
and
h
t − 1
to evaluate and adjust the degree of forgetting of the last-time cell state
C
t − 1
. Then, an update of the cell state is completed by calculating the new cell state
C t
due to the two gate outputs (
f t
,
i t
) and last-time cell state
C
t − 1
, which decides what information to throw away, and what new information to add. The output gate decides what the next hidden state should be by calculating weights and bias
{
0 ≤
(
W
x o
,
W
h o
,
b o
)
≤ 1
}
for the given
x t
and
h
t − 1
. The hidden state
h t
contains information about previous inputs, and is used for predictions. The hidden state is updated by calculating a new hidden state through Eq. (5), where the symbol
⨀
means the elementwise product of vectors. The tanh function ensures that the values stay in the range of −1 to 1, and multiplying it by the output-gate output
o t
determines what information the hidden state should carry forward:
(1)
i t
= σ ⁡
(
W
x i
·
x t
+
W
h i
·
h
t − 1
+
b i
)
(2)
f t
= σ ⁡
(
W
x f
·
x t
+
W
h f
·
h
t − 1
+
b f
)
(3)
o t
= σ
(
W
x o
·
x t
+
W
h o
·
h
t − 1
+
b o
)
(4)
C t
=
f t
C
t − 1
+
i t
tanh ⁡
(
W
x c
·
x t
+
W
h c
·
h
t − 1
+
b c
)
(5)
h t
=
o t
⨀
tanh ⁡
(
C t
)

These formulae, working in concert, allow LSTM cells to make complex decisions about what information to keep, discard, or pass on. This means that the LSTM deep-learning model is particularly suitable for handling time-series data of long sequences. However, as discussed in section 2.2, the dynamic nonlinear variability of the battery's time-series data (i.e., statistical discord between current and voltage values) more seriously aggravates the problem of overfitting in training LSTM networks, which often leads to lower-than-expected accuracy for the drastic change of unlearned data. Accordingly, the design of new LSTM network models is needed, which is applicable to estimating stable and accurate SOC, even in the time-series data of the EV-battery with dynamic nonlinear characteristics.

The SOC estimation using the LSTN model displays the greatest weakness of nonlinearity between current and voltage values in the time-series, which is attributed to the nonlinear and time-varying response characteristics of batteries during charging and discharging, as mentioned above. This is because the correlated voltage values are actively reflected in learning, while uncorrelated current values are considered as dynamic random fluctuations in the time-series. However, since the LSTM deep-learning model is particularly suited to handling the battery's time-series data of long sequences, the time-varying variability of current causing the voltage change should without question be considered in the learning processes. Hence, to overcome the weakness of the LSTM model, this paper introduces a new dual LSTM network model that adds a variability-learning LSTM module in parallel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A dual LSTM network architecture in combination with g–LSTM
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed collaborative model in this work has a dual LSTM network structure that consists of mainstream (m–) LSTM and gradient (g–) LSTM in parallel, and finally, it possesses a dense block of fine-tuning m–LSTM output features with g–LSTM output features. Fig. 4 indicates the overall structure of the proposed dual LSTM network, where M, N,
v m
, and
v g
denote the numbers of m–LSTM and g–LSTM unit-cells, and their output features, respectively. Specifically, the m–LSTM captures magnitude-value-oriented dependencies (
v m
) of input sequences, while the g–LSTM, possessing a gradient (g–) function, extracts data-variability features (
v g
) characterizing the dynamic nonlinear behavior of the input data. After that, the dense block calculates a SOC estimate of
S O C
p r e
after fine-tuning
v m
with
v g
, as described in Eqs. (6) and (7):
(6)
v ˜
o
= R e L U
(
W
0 , m
·
v m
+
W
0 , g
·
v g
+
b 0
)
(7)
S O C
p r e
=
W 2
· R e L U
(
W 1
·
v ˜
o
+
b 1
)
+
b 2
where, the weight and bias
{
W k
and
b k
, k = 0 , 1 , 2
}
in the
k
− t h
layer are updated while training, and are optimized to enhance the model's performance. The ReLU denotes a rectified linear unit that is an activation function [32]. The layer comprising two dense layers and an output layer performs a merging process that involves fine-tuning and SOC prediction. For this purpose, 0
−th dense layer includes the nodes that output (M + N)/32 tuned vectors (
v ˜
o
) from M and N output features (
v m
,
v g
) of m–LSTM and g–LSTM modules. In the following 1
−th dense layer the number of nodes is merged to yield (M + N)/64 vectors for the performance enhancement of
v ˜
o
, and finally a
S O C
p r e
is outputted in the output layer. The proposed methodology includes two calculation modules of m–LSTM and g–LSTM to capture the data-magnitude long dependency and time-varying dynamic variability, respectively, of the battery's time-series data. The g–LSTM allows enough information to learn the random, dynamic, and nonlinear response of batteries, without building a larger model with many parameters. The proposed method lets the model use shorter input sequences with relatively higher stability, thus making the model smaller, and yet, more accurate.

In the proposed dual LSTM network, the g–LSTM module strengthens the weak point of the LSTM, which often suffers difficulty in extracting nonlinear and dynamic features from the time-series data of a battery during repeated charging and discharging. For this purpose, the g–LSTM characterizes the dynamic and nonlinear variability of the time-series input data through the g–function, and learns variability features for tuning m–LSTM features. Fig. 5 indicates a g–LSTM cell structure containing a gradient (g–) function that generates the dynamic variation information from input data. In this work, the g–function is derived from a closed-form polynomial of order 2K, as below [33,34]:
(8a)
G
( z )
=
(
2 − z −
z
− 1
4
)
{
∑
l = 0
K − 1
d
K , l
(
2 + z +
z
− 1
4
)
l
}
where,
d
K , l
is
(8b)
d
K , l
=
(
K + l − 1
l
)
=
(
K + l − 1
)
!
(
K − 1
)
! × l !
The g–function
G
( z )
is a closed-form polynomial having a maximum number of zeros at z = 1, and thus, the frequency response
G
( z )
|
z =
e
j ω
has a maximally flat response at ω = 0, i.e.,
(9)
∂ k
G
(
e
j ω
)
∂
ω k
|
ω = π
= 0 , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 K − 1

The
G
( z )
of Eq. (8) represents a symmetric FIR high-pass filter with a maximally flat magnitude characteristic in the stopband, and allows explicit formulae for the direct computation of filter coefficients [26]. The output of the
G
( z )
, along with the hidden state from the previous time-step, are sent to each gate and node for further processing, so that the dynamic variability information is applied to the whole calculation.

Fig. 6 shows the stochastic distribution of gradient (g–) input features
δ
x t
(
δ
V i
, δ
I t
)
in the V−I coordinate plane, in which
δ
V i
and
δ
I t
are obtained by using the g–function of Eq. (8) with K = 2. Further, Table 5 indicates the related data, where {
m u
,
σ v
|
u , v = δ I , δ V
} denote the mean and variance, respectively, of
δ
I t
and
δ
V t
. In this regard,
δ
V i
and
δ
I t
can be said to represent the current and voltage variabilities, since they correspond to the high-pass components of I and V. These results show that the current- and voltage-gradient values exhibit a narrow bell-shape distribution that is zero-centered and symmetric with SDs of (
σ
δ I
,
σ
δ V
) = (0.03, 0.29), respectively, regardless of the profile type and temperature condition. Then, approximately 75.63–92.83 % and 79.28–93.9 % of
δ
I t
and
δ
V t
fall within one SD of the mean, respectively, and have zero or sufficiently negligible value. This means that the g–function plays a significant role in characterizing the nonlinear and time-varying variability of the input feature data
x t
(
V t
,
I t
)
; by this means, the valid variability-training data actually participating in the g–LSTM learning is only 6.1–24.37 %. Ultimately, the g–LSTM by learning the gradient information contributes to mapping the dynamic variation of the input data of the battery for accurate SOC estimation, which helps maintain the stability of the overall performance, even for the random nonlinear dynamic response of the battery. For more analysis of the g–LSTM learning features, Table 6 shows the power summations of
δ
x t
(
δ
V t
, δ
I t
)
overall, within and without one-SD range. Table 6 also shows that the power summation of the g–voltage values falling within one-SD range is lower than the value of 1.0, regardless of the profile type or temperature condition, which is due to their high autocorrelation (>0.95 in time-lag τ = 1) and information redundancy, as mentioned in Table 2. Remarkably, the g–voltage values
δ
V t
within one-SD range, taking up approximately 83.2 % (on average of 79.28–93.9 %) of the total voltage data against just 5.475 % (on average of 2.9–8.05 %) of its total power, have an ignorable effect on the learning error rate, since they are either zero value or of near-zero value (less than 10−6, similar to noise). On the other hand, it can be found that the power summation of g–current values
δ
I t
in each probability distribution range is sufficiently larger as a whole than that of the voltage, and increases slightly with increasing temperature, indicating that the variability of the current is more dynamic and pervasive.

Consequently, these results suggest that the g–LSTM focuses on learning the dynamic variability of the current having relatively larger value than that of the voltage in the time-series, which can contribute to improving the accuracy of SOC, even though using unlearned data.

Reason for m–LSTM collaboration with g–LSTM in parallel : As illustrated in Fig. 6 and Tables 5 and 6, most of the current and voltage gradient values corresponding to approximately 75.63–93.9 % have a smaller value close to zero; this means that there is a significant difference in overall bias between the original input data and the gradient feature data. This large bias difference disturbs the optimization of the serial-structured combination model between m–LSTM and g–LSTM modules, which is attributed to different stochastic characteristics by different data type. Note that during backpropagation, the weights of all the layers will be randomly initialized and updated using the same loss flow from the output [17]. So, at the beginning of the training, the data with relatively high bias plays a greater role in the output generation than the data with low bias close to 0, making the loss (or gradient) more focused on the higher-biased input data. This makes it hard for a model to fully consider the gradient-tuning features when they are calculated with shared parameter. So, as illustrated above, designing the g–LSTM to deal with the original input and dynamic variability together and building a serial model cannot be an option.

The vanilla LSTM is well-known for its ability to deal with relatively long sequences, like battery data on-site, but using long current sequences with random dynamic variability rather disturbs the performance of SOC prediction. Hence, a new LSTM-based model that considers current variability characteristics is needed. To address the difficulty from the tendency, a dual LSTM with a reliable and robust structure is proposed in this work, which combines m–LSTM with g–LSTM in parallel. As mentioned above, the gradient feature vectors are extracted independently, so that the loss flow during backpropagation splits through m–LSTM and g–LSTM, respectively. The extracted gradient feature vectors are incorporated into a dense layer to assign weights to the output features of m–LSTM, which helps maintain stable model performance for the random nonlinear dynamic response of the battery. Fig. 7 represents the parallel structure of m–LSTM and g–LSTM in the proposed network model, where M and N denote the numbers of m–LSTM and g–LSTM unit cells, respectively. Consequently, in the proposed model, the m–LSTM as a general type extracts the overall data-intensity (magnitude value) oriented features, whereas the g–LSTM learns and extracts gradient features that characterize the time-series variability of the battery data.

In this section, the learning performance of the proposed m–LSTM/g–LSTM dual network is evaluated and compared through various experiments. In addition, before discussing the results, the dual network model is developed for a number of cell pairs (M, N) in the m–LSTM and g–LSTM network. Finally, the proposed method demonstrates that the g–LSTM collaboration can allow a strong and robust solution to estimate accurate SOC, without additional computation and structure requirements. To that end, all LSTM learning models used for testing in this work were trained for 1200 epochs at batch size of 128, along with learning rate of 0.001 with the Adam optimizer [35]. Further, all experiments were carried out using DST and US06 sets for training, and the FUDS set for validation, respectively. To show the effectiveness of the g–LSTM collaboration in the LSTM network, i.e., how well the proposed dual LSTM model can predict the SOC, the SOC estimation error between the predicted value and the true reference was evaluated through three widely used metrics, namely, the mean absolute error (MAE), mean square error (MSE), and root mean square error (RMSE), as follow:
(10a)
M A E =
1 K
∑
i = 1
K
|
S O C
i
p r e
−
S O C
i
r e f
|
(10b)
M S E =
1 K
∑
i = 1
K
(
S O C
i
p r e
−
S O C
i
r e f
)
2
(10c)
R M S E =
1 K
∑
i = 1
K
(
S O C
i
p r e
−
S O C
i
r e f
)
2
where, K is the total number of samples in the dataset,
S O C
i
p r e
is the predicted value, and
S O C
i
r e f
is the actual true value.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the m–LSTM/g–LSTM collaboration network, the building of the proposed deep learning model first requires the training options to be decided, as follow: (a) input sequence length (suitable to the proposed dual model), and (b) the number of g–LSTM cells over m–LSTM. The sequence length, which is the number of time-steps input into the machine learning network, is critical in LSTM models; hence, this sequence size fine-tuning must be conducted based on the performance observation. This is due to the highly uncorrelated random variability of the input features caused by the current data of battery charging and discharging (as shown in section 2). Accordingly, in learning the time-series data of the battery system, the input sequence length should be carefully decided upon, which is bound up with the SOC estimation accuracy, as well as the LSTM computational complexity. In specifying the number of cells in LSTM, there is no final, definite rule of thumb on how many nodes or how many layers should be chosen, and very often, a trial-and-error approach can provide the best results for a given LSTM network model. Furthermore, the proposed dual model should provide each number of m–LSTM and g–LSTM cells, (M, N) defined in Fig. 8 , to build a successful network combination model for accurate SOC estimation. This section suggests how the numbers of m–LSTM and g–LSTM cells are experimentally determined based on the stochastic distribution characteristics of their data types, as illustrated in Figs. 2 and 6.

While there is no strict upper limit on the number of time-steps that can be specified with a sequence length of the time-varying dynamic data of the battery, practical considerations, such as computational resources and specific tasks at hand, play an important role in determining the effective range for accurate SOC estimation. In fact, in the case of highly correlated sequences, LSTM models have been shown to capture long dependencies over hundreds of time-steps [36]. For such reasons, the influence of sequence length of the battery's time-series data on the performance of SOC estimation in a single-layer vanilla LSTM model of 256 unit-cells was first investigated [17,28], before evaluating the proposed dual LSTM. The performance evaluation was carried out for various sequence lengths ranging (20–60), in steps of 10. Fig. 8 shows the experimental results of three average errors of Eq. (10) from 10 runs for each sequence length, using the battery's data set of 25 °C. In addition, Table 7 presents the related data. Shorter sequence lengths tend to yield higher errors, which is attributed to insufficient information from the input data for SOC estimation. On the other hand, it appears that excessively longer sequence lengths lead to reduction in accuracy of the LSTM, due to the increase in time-varying nonlinearity between current and voltage values [37]. The experimental result suggests that the sequence length of 40 exhibits the best accuracy of SOC estimation, resulting in MAE of 1.0117 %, MSE of 1.6425 %, and RMSE of 1.2809 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the number of g–LSTM unit cells over m–LSTM
ce:section.ce:para: To design the proposed dual LSTM single-layer model shown in Fig. 7, the important task of determining the number of m–LSTM and g–LSTM cells separately remains. This is because their two input sequences have different stochastic distribution characteristics with different dynamic variability, as indicated in Figs. 2 and 6. In this work, the split number of the m–LSTM and g–LSTM cells for a given total 256 unit cells was determined by the experimental results of 10 runs [17,28]. Further, DST/US06 and FUDS datasets at 25 °C for training and testing, respectively, were used. Fig. 9 displays the average SOC estimation errors of 10 runs according to the split number of 256 unit-cells, while Table 8 presents the related data. These show that a (248, 8) pair of split m–LSTM and g–LSTM cells exhibits the lowest estimation error among the tested pairs. In the case of the (248, 8) dual LSTM model, as described in Table 9 , the number of parameters represents a decrease of about 5.95 % by introducing the g–LSTM with 8 unit-cells, as compared to the single layer vanilla LSTM model of 256 unit cells [28,38].

To show the effectiveness of the proposed g–LSTM collaboration, the (248, 8)-pair dual LSTM was compared to vanilla LSTM model containing a single layer of 256 cells and the Transformer, another promising deep learning model. As mentioned earlier, both dual and vanilla LSTM models were trained for 1200 epochs using batch size of 128, sequence length of 40, and learning rate of 0.001, where Adam was adopted as an optimizer [36]. In the case of Transformer, model structure contains 4 encoder layers, 4 decoder layers, and 10 attention heads according to the normal settings, and it was trained for 1200 epochs using batch size of 128, sequence length of 40, and learning rate of 0.0003. The hardware used for all experiments are Intel(R) Core™ i5-12400F, 16 GB Memory, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060. Model construction and training were carried out using TensorFlow 2.9.1, Python 3.10.6, cuDNN 8.5.0 and CUDA 11.7. Further, all evaluations were performed by using DST and US06 sets for training, and FUDS set for validation, respectively.

Fig. 10 compares the average SOC estimation errors from 10 runs for each (0, 25, and 45) °C, respectively while Table 10 presents the related data with computational time for training and inference. These show that the proposed dual model displays improved SOC estimation accuracy caused by g–LSTM collaboration, which leads to 4.1 (13.5) % lower MAE, 10.4 (18.1) % lower MSE, and 5.3 (9.5) % lower RMSE, respectively, at 25 °C, than the vanilla LSTM (Transformer) model. In the case of comparison with vanilla LSTM, the SOC estimation errors at low temperature of 0 °C can be found to be reduced dramatically by (15.4, 21.8, and 11.3) % in MAE, MSE, and RMSE, respectively. Moreover, as compared to the Transformer, the proposed model exhibits more apparently high-performance enhancement at 45 °C, resulting in 38.5 % lower MAE, 52.1 % lower MSE, and 30.8 % lower RMSE, respectively. These results demonstrate that the proposed method not only provides excellent performance with lower computational cost but also has a remarkable advantage that it enables precise SOC estimation utilizing only voltage and current data on site, while the Transformer model has complex architecture that should necessitate supplementary SOC data (for decoder) in practical electric vehicle applications after model training. Fig. 11 compares the SOC curves between the estimates and references, where Fig. 11(a) and (b) compare the SOC reference and estimate of the lowest MAE among 10 runs of the dual-LSTM and vanilla LSTM models. This shows that the vanilla LSTM exhibits larger fluctuation errors, where the errors of the dual LSTM can be limited to a narrow range of ±1.32 %. Fig. 11(c) and (d) illustrate the SOC curves of both models trained with 2400 epochs under identical conditions. These results demonstrate enhanced accuracy in the mid-discharge phase of SOC, which is typically challenging to estimate with precision due to the flat open-circuit voltage (OCV) curve [39–41]. Such performance enhancement of the proposed model is attributed to the data-gradient output features of the g–LSTM contributing to fine-tuning the output features of m–LSTM in the dense layer, which helps maintain the stable overall performance, even on unlearned data. These results suggest that the proposed dual LSTM model is suitable for accurate SOC prediction of the time-series data of the EV-battery with dynamic nonlinear and time-varying characteristics.

Based on the comprehensive analysis presented in this paper, a novel dual-mode LSTM network architecture has been successfully developed for the accurate estimation of the state of charge (SOC) in lithium–ion batteries of electric vehicles. The proposed model combining a mainstream (m–) LSTM and gradient (g–) LSTM in parallel demonstrates a highly effective and robust structure that enables the capture of the data magnitude-oriented dependency and time-varying dynamic variability of the battery's time-series data, respectively. The g–LSTM module, accounting for only a small fraction of the total LSTM cells, significantly enhances the model's performance by fine-tuning the m–LSTM output features in dense block, and helps provide a stable performance on unlearned data. The experimental results have demonstrated that the proposed dual model exhibits remarkably enhanced SOC estimation accuracy caused by g–LSTM collaboration, which leads to 4.1 (13.5) % lower MAE, 10.4 (18.1) % lower MSE and 5.3 (9.5) % lower RMSE at 25 °C, than the vanilla LSTM (Transformer) model, respectively. In the case of comparison with vanilla LSTM, the SOC estimation errors at low temperature of 0 °C are reduced dramatically by (15.4, 21.8, and 11.3) % in MAE, MSE and RMSE, respectively. Moreover, as compared to the Transformer, the proposed model exhibits more apparently high-performance enhancement at 45 °C, resulting in 38.5 % lower MAE, 52.1 % lower MSE, and 30.8 % lower RMSE, respectively. Nevertheless, the dual-mode structure including the collaboration of g–LSTM using fewer unit cells ensures faster convergence of the training procedure (faster reach of the minimum error). In this approach, the proposed dual LSTM network represents a significant advancement in SOC estimation, offering a robust and efficient tool to ensure the safety and longevity of lithium–ion batteries in electric vehicles.

Junyoung Ahn: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Yoonseok Lee: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Byeongjik Han: Validation, Investigation. Sohyeon Lee: Software, Resources. Yunsun Kim: Software, Investigation. Daewon Chung: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Joonhyeon Jeon: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP), and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20224000000020). This work was also supported by the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT), Korea, under the ICT Challenge and Advanced Network of HRD (ICAN) support program (IITP-2024-RS-2024-00437186), supervised by the Institute for Information & Communications Technology Planning & Evaluation (IITP).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rectified linear units improve restricted Boltzmann machines
Proceedings of the 27th international conference on machine learning

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Nair, Vinod, and Geoffrey E. Hinton. “Rectified linear units improve restricted Boltzmann machines.” Proceedings of the 27th international conference on machine learning (ICML-10). 2010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of FIR half-band filter with controllable transition-band steepness
IEEE Access

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cho, Woon, et al. “Design of FIR half-band filter with controllable transition-band steepness.” IEEE Access 9 (2021): 52144-52154, https://doi.org/10.1109/access.2021.3069985

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of cut off-frequency fixing filters by error compensation of MAXFLAT FIR filters
Electronics

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Adam: a method for stochastic optimization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kingma, Diederik P., and Jimmy Ba. “Adam: A method for stochastic optimization.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1412.6980 (2014), https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1412.6980

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel battery SOC estimation method based on random search optimized LSTM neural network
Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xuqing Chai, Shihao Li, Fengwei Liang, “A novel battery SOC estimation method based on random search optimized LSTM neural network”, Energy, 306 (2024) 132583, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.energy.2024.132583

Proceedings of the IEEE international conference on computer vision

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: He, Kaiming, et al. “Delving deep into rectifiers: Surpassing human-level performance on imagenet classification.” Proceedings of the IEEE international conference on computer vision. 2015, https://doi.org/10.1109/iccv.2015.123

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fault diagnosis of hydro-turbine via the incorporation of bayesian algorithm optimized CNN-LSTM neural network
Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fang Dao, Yun Zeng, Jing Qian, “Fault diagnosis of hydro-turbine via the incorporation of bayesian algorithm optimized CNN-LSTM neural network”, Energy 290 (2024), 130326 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.energy.2024.130326

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State-of-charge estimation of LiFePO4 batteries in electric vehicles: a deep-learning enabled approach
Appl Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jinepng Tian, Rui Xiong, Weixiang Shen, Juahuan Lu, “State-of-charge estimation of LiFePO4 batteries in electric vehicles: A deep-learning enabled approach”, Applied Energy 291 (2021), 116812 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apenergy.2021.116812

Energy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State of charge estimation for lithium-ion batteries based on battery model and data-driven fusion method
Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiayang Hou, Jun Xu, Chuanping Lin, Delong Jiang, Xuesong Mei, “State of charge estimation for lithium-ion batteries based on battery model and data-driven fusion method”, Energy 290 (2024), 130056 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.energy.2023.130056
